go:546.title: GATE CSE 1992 | Question: 01,ii
go:546.question: Consider a $3$-bit error detection and $1$-bit error correction hamming code for $4$-bit data. The extra parity bits required would be _____ and the $3$-bit error detection is possible because the code has a minimum distance of _______.

go:551.title: GATE CSE 1992 | Question: 01-vi
go:551.question: In an $11$-bit computer instruction format, the size of address field is $4$-bits. The computer uses expanding OP code technique and has $5$ two-address instructions and $32$ one-address instructions. The number of zero-address instructions it can support is ________

go:555.title: GATE CSE 1992 | Question: 02-i
go:555.question:
The operation which is commutative but not associative is:

  1. AND
  2. OR
  3. EX-OR
  4. NAND

go:556.title: GATE CSE 1992 | Question: 02-ii
go:556.question:
All digital circuits can be realized using only

  1. Ex-OR gates
  2. Multiplexers
  3. Half adders
  4. OR gates

go:568.title: GATE CSE 1992 | Question: 02-xi
go:568.question:
A computer system has $6$ tape devices, with n processes competing for them. Each process may need $3$ tape drives. The maximum value of n for which the system is guaranteed to be deadlock-free is:

  1. $2$
  2. $3$
  3. $4$
  4. $1$

go:569.title: GATE CSE 1992 | Question: 02-xii
go:569.question:
Which of the following is an example of a spooled device?

  1. The terminal used to input data for a program being executed.
  2. The secondary memory device in a virtual memory system
  3. A line printer used to print the output of a number of jobs.
  4. None of the above

go:570.title: GATE CSE 1992 | Question: 02,xiii
go:570.question:
For a context free grammar, FOLLOW(A) is the set of terminals that can appear immediately to the right of non-terminal $A$ in some "sentential" form. We define two sets LFOLLOW(A) and RFOLLOW(A) by replacing the word "sentential" by "left sentential" and "right most sentential" respectively in the definition of FOLLOW (A).

  1. FOLLOW(A) and LFOLLOW(A) may be different.
  2. FOLLOW(A) and RFOLLOW(A) are always the same.
  3. All the three sets are identical.
  4. All the three sets are different.

go:584.title: GATE CSE 1992 | Question: 5-a
go:584.question: The access times of the main memory and the Cache memory, in a computer system, are $500$ n sec and $50$ nsec, respectively. It is estimated that $80\%$ of the main memory request are for read the rest for write. The hit ratio for the read access only is $0.9$ and a write-through policy (where both main and cache memories are updated simultaneously) is used. Determine the average time of the main memory (in ns).

go:43582.title: GATE CSE 1992 | Question: 12-b
go:43582.question: Let the page reference and the working set window be $c\ c\ d\ b\ c\ e\ c\ e\ a\ d\ $ and $4$, respectively. The initial working set at time $t=0$ contains the pages $\{a,d,e\}$, where $a$ was referenced at time $t=0$, $d$ was referenced at time $t=-1$, and $e$ was referenced at time $t=-2$. Determine the total number of page faults and the average number of page frames used by computing the working set at each reference.

go:43580.title: GATE CSE 1992 | Question: 14b
go:43580.question:
Consider the set of integers $\{1,2,3,4,6,8,12,24\}$ together with the two binary operations LCM (lowest common multiple) and GCD (greatest common divisor). Which of the following algebraic structures does this represent?

  1. group
  2. ring
  3. field
  4. lattice

go:595.title: GATE CSE 1992 | Question: 16
go:595.question:
Which of the following three statements are true? Prove your answer.

  1. The union of two recursive languages is recursive.
  2. The language $\{O^n \mid n\text{ is a prime} \}$ is not regular.
  3. Regular languages are closed under infinite union.

go:256.title: GATE CSE 1992 | Question: 92,xv
go:256.question:
Which of the following predicate calculus statements is/are valid?

  1. $(\forall (x)) P(x) \vee (\forall(x))Q(x) \implies (\forall (x)) (P(x) \vee Q(x))$
  2. $(\exists (x)) P(x) \wedge (\exists (x))Q(x) \implies (\exists (x)) (P(x) \wedge Q(x))$
  3. $(\forall (x)) (P(x) \vee Q(x)) \implies (\forall (x)) P(x) \vee (\forall (x)) Q(x)$
  4. $(\exists (x)) (P(x) \vee Q(x)) \implies \sim (\forall (x)) P(x) \vee (\exists (x)) Q(x)$
